source.title: Virological characteristics of the SARS-CoV-2 XBB variant derived from recombination of two Omicron subvariants

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The study investigates the virological characteristics of the SARS-CoV-2 XBB variant, a recombinant lineage from two Omicron subvariants (BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1). The problem is to understand how recombination impacts viral fitness, immune evasion, receptor binding, fusogenicity, and pathogenicity, and to explain XBB's rapid global spread.

section_gists.heading: Evolution and epidemics of the XBB variant
section_gists.gist: XBB emerged via recombination between BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 in summer 2022, with a breakpoint in the RBD. XBB's effective reproduction number (Re) is higher than parental lineages, marking the first known fitness increase via recombination. XBB and BQ.1 lineages dominate in different regions but have similar Re.
process_signals: Recombination identified as a novel mechanism for fitness increase in SARS-CoV-2.
Regional dominance patterns suggest geographic spread dynamics.
methods: Phylogenetic analysis using GISAID sequences
Recombination detection with RDP5
Bayesian tip-dated phylogenetic inference for tMRCA estimation
Effective reproduction number (Re) estimation from epidemic data
metrics: XBB Re 1.23- and 1.20-times higher than BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1
XBB and XBB.1 Re 1.24- and 1.26-times higher than BA.5

section_gists.heading: Immune resistance of XBB.1
section_gists.gist: XBB.1 shows profound resistance to neutralization by sera from BA.2 and BA.5 breakthrough infections, with 30-fold resistance to BA.2 sera. Multiple substitutions (e.g., V83A, Y144del, R346T, F486S, F490S) contribute cooperatively. XBB.1 evades therapeutic antibodies except sotrovimab.
process_signals: Hypothesis: XBB.1 has enhanced immune evasion due to accumulated mutations.
Observation: Profound resistance confirmed; many sera fail to neutralize.
Partial result: Some therapeutic antibodies retain activity (sotrovimab).
methods: HIV-1-based pseudovirus neutralization assay
Single substitution mutants in BA.2 background
Testing against convalescent sera, vaccinee sera, and monoclonal antibodies
metrics: Fold resistance for individual substitutions (e.g., F486S: 3.0-fold)
Neutralization titers (NT50) against various sera and antibodies
failures_or_limits: Most therapeutic antibodies ineffective except sotrovimab
Limited sample size for sera (14 BA.2, 20 BA.5)

section_gists.heading: ACE2 binding affinity of XBB.1 S
section_gists.gist: XBB.1 RBD has higher ACE2 binding affinity (KD=1.0 nM) than BA.2 (1.5 nM). F486S reduces affinity (2.8 nM) but is compensated by other substitutions. XBB.1 pseudovirus shows 8-fold higher infectivity than BA.2 in HOS-ACE2/TMPRSS2 cells.
process_signals: Hypothesis: Altered ACE2 binding may affect infectivity.
Observation: Increased overall affinity despite F486S reducing binding.
Trade-off: F486S aids immune evasion but reduces ACE2 binding; compensated by other mutations.
methods: Yeast surface display for KD measurement
Pseudovirus infectivity assay in HOS-ACE2/TMPRSS2 cells
metrics: KD: BA.2=1.5 nM, XBB.1=1.0 nM, F486S=2.8 nM

section_gists.heading: Fusogenicity of XBB.1 S
section_gists.gist: XBB.1 S is more fusogenic than BA.2 (2.2-fold) and BA.2.75 (1.5-fold) despite lower surface expression. Substitutions V83A and R346T increase fusogenicity; N460K also contributes (from BA.2.75 background).
process_signals: Hypothesis: Fusogenicity may correlate with pathogenicity.
Observation: Increased fusogenicity despite lower S surface expression.
Unexpected: Lower surface expression but higher fusion activity.
methods: SARS-CoV-2 S-based fusion assay in Calu-3 cells
Surface expression quantification by flow cytometry
metrics: Fusogenicity: XBB.1 2.2-fold vs BA.2, 1.5-fold vs BA.2.75
Surface expression: XBB.1 significantly lower than BA.2 and BA.2.75

section_gists.heading: Structural characteristics of XBB.1 S
section_gists.gist: Cryo-EM structures show XBB.1 S predominantly in closed states, with RBD one-up state rare. F486S loses hydrophobic interactions with ACE2, explaining reduced affinity. F490S alters interaction with ACE2 K31. N103-glycan of ACE2 positioned unusually toward active site.
process_signals: Hypothesis: Structural changes underlie altered binding and immune evasion.
Observation: Closed state preference may affect receptor engagement.
Mechanistic insight: F486S and F490S changes explain binding trade-offs.
methods: Cryo-EM of XBB.1 S ectodomain alone and in complex with ACE2
Local refinement for RBD-ACE2 interface
metrics: Resolutions: closed-1 2.50 Å, closed-2 2.51 Å, RBD-ACE2 3.29 Å
Structural comparisons with BA.2.75 and BQ.1.1

section_gists.heading: Virological characteristics of XBB.1 in vitro
section_gists.gist: XBB.1 growth kinetics are comparable to BA.2.75 in most cell lines but lower in iPSC-derived alveolar epithelial cells. XBB.1 disrupts airway epithelial-endothelial barriers more than BA.2.75, consistent with higher fusogenicity.
process_signals: Hypothesis: In vitro replication fitness reflects in vivo transmissibility.
Observation: Variable replication efficiency across cell types.
Partial result: Lower replication in alveolar cells but higher barrier disruption.
methods: Virus inoculation in Vero, Calu-3, AO-ALI, iPSC-derived airway/alveolar cells, VeroE6/TMPRSS2
Airway-on-a-chip system for barrier disruption assay
metrics: Viral RNA and infectious titers over time
Percentage of virus invading bottom channel in airway-on-chip
failures_or_limits: XBB.1 less replicative than BA.2.75 in iPSC-derived alveolar epithelial cells

section_gists.heading: Virological characteristics of XBB.1 in vivo
section_gists.gist: In hamsters, XBB.1 causes no weight loss and respiratory parameters similar to BA.2.75, less pathogenic than Delta. Viral RNA loads in lungs and nasal turbinates are comparable to BA.2.75. XBB.1 shows efficient transmission to co-housed hamsters.
process_signals: Hypothesis: XBB.1 pathogenicity is attenuated relative to Delta.
Observation: Pathogenicity comparable to BA.2.75, lower than Delta.
Validation: Transmission efficiency confirmed.
methods: Hamster infection with clinical isolates
Body weight, respiratory function (Penh, Rpef) measurement
Viral RNA quantification in tissues
Transmission experiment with co-housing
metrics: Body weight change, Penh, Rpef values
Viral RNA copies in lung and nasal turbinate
failures_or_limits: Hamster model may not fully recapitulate human disease

section_gists.heading: Intrinsic pathogenicity of XBB.1
section_gists.gist: Histopathological analysis of hamster lungs shows XBB.1 causes less damage than Delta, with scores comparable to BA.2.75. Parameters include bronchitis, alveolar damage, and type II pneumocyte hyperplasia.
process_signals: Hypothesis: XBB.1 has reduced intrinsic pathogenicity.
Observation: Confirmed lower pathogenicity than Delta, similar to BA.2.75.
methods: Histopathological scoring of formalin-fixed lung sections
Four-tiered scoring system for five parameters
metrics: Scores for bronchitis, hemorrhage, alveolar damage, type II pneumocytes, hyperplasia area

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: XBB is the first SARS-CoV-2 variant with fitness increased by recombination. Its profound immune evasion is due to cooperative mutations, including NTD Y144del. Despite high fusogenicity, pathogenicity is attenuated, suggesting a trade-off. Regional dominance may be due to geographic spread rather than immune status. Limitations include sample size and in vitro vs. in vivo differences.
process_signals: Synthesis: Recombination as a novel fitness mechanism.
Trade-off: High immune evasion and fusogenicity but reduced pathogenicity.
Revision: Regional differences likely due to founder effects, not immune landscape.
Limitations acknowledged: Small sample sizes, model systems.
failures_or_limits: In vitro findings may not fully translate in vivo
Hamster model may not reflect human pathogenicity
Need for further studies on transmission and immune escape

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Recombination between Omicron subvariants can generate variants with enhanced fitness and immune evasion.
XBB.1's immune resistance is driven by cooperative effects of multiple spike mutations, including NTD changes.
Increased ACE2 binding affinity compensates for immune evasion mutations that reduce receptor binding.
Higher fusogenicity does not necessarily correlate with increased pathogenicity, indicating a trade-off.
XBB's global spread is facilitated by its fitness advantage, with regional dominance patterns reflecting founder effects.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Whether the trade-off between immune evasion and pathogenicity will persist in future variants.
The role of non-spike mutations in XBB's fitness.
Long-term effectiveness of current vaccines and therapeutics against XBB sublineages.
Impact of XBB's altered ACE2 glycan interaction on viral entry and tropism.
Generalizability of hamster pathogenicity results to human disease severity.

events.summary: The study aims to investigate the virological characteristics of the XBB variant, which emerged through recombination of two Omicron subvariants.
Phylogenetic analysis suggests XBB emerged through recombination of BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 during summer 2022.
XBB shows higher effective reproduction number (Re) than parental lineages, indicating increased fitness through recombination.
numbers_or_metrics: Re of XBB 1.23- and 1.20-times higher than BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1
XBB and XBB.1 Re 1.24- and 1.26-times higher than BA.5

events.summary: XBB.1 exhibits profound resistance to neutralization by BA.2 breakthrough infection sera (30-fold).
Multiple single substitutions in XBB.1 S protein confer significant resistance to BA.2 breakthrough sera.
methods_or_tools: BA.2 S mutants with single substitutions

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers the introduction, evolution/epidemics, and part of immune resistance results. It contains key problem statement, phylogenetic analysis, fitness estimation, and initial immune evasion experiments.

events.summary: XBB.1 exhibits profound resistance to breakthrough BA.5 infection sera, with 13-fold resistance compared to BA.2.
Multiple substitutions in XBB.1 S cooperatively contribute to immune resistance against BA.2 breakthrough infection sera.
Y144del and G446S mutations cooperatively contribute to resistance against BA.5 breakthrough infection sera.
XBB.1 significantly escapes from 4-dose vaccine sera, including monovalent, BA.1 bivalent, and BA.5 bivalent vaccines.
XBB.1 is antigenically distinct from other Omicron subvariants, including BQ.1.1, based on hamster sera neutralization and antigenic cartography.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains immune resistance and ACE2 binding affinity results for XBB.1, with explicit experimental data and statistical comparisons.

events.summary: Identified R346T, L368I, and N460K as substitutions enhancing ACE2 binding affinity, while F486S decreases it.
Assessed pseudovirus infectivity, showing XBB.1 pseudovirus has 7.6-fold greater infectivity than BA.2.
Determined that R346T, L368I, and V83A significantly increase pseudovirus infectivity, while Y144del and G252V decrease it.
Measured fusogenicity of XBB.1 S using S-based fusion assay, finding it 2.2-fold more fusogenic than BA.2 S and 1.5-fold more than BA.2.75 S.
Identified V83A, R346T, and N460K as substitutions contributing to augmented fusogenicity of XBB.1 S.
Determined cryo-EM structures of XBB.1 S ectodomain alone (closed-1 and closed-2 states) and in complex with ACE2 (one-up and two-up conformations).
Observed that F486S loses hydrophobic interactions with ACE2, explaining reduced binding affinity, and F490S alters interaction with ACE2 K31.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed experimental results on ACE2 binding, pseudovirus infectivity, fusogenicity, and cryo-EM structures, all directly supporting the paper's investigation of XBB.1 virological characteristics.

events.summary: XBB.1 growth kinetics compared to BA.2 and BA.2.75 in multiple cell lines, showing comparable growth except in iPSC-derived alveolar epithelial cells where XBB.1 is less replicative.
Airway-on-a-chip system used to assess disruption of airway epithelial-endothelial barrier by XBB.1, showing significantly higher invasion than BA.2.75.
Results suggest XBB.1 is more fusogenic than BA.2.75, consistent with S-based fusion assay.
Hamster inoculation with Delta, BA.2.75, and XBB.1 to investigate in vivo pathogenicity; XBB.1 shows no weight loss and respiratory parameters comparable to BA.2.75, less pathogenic than Delta.
Viral RNA load in oral swabs and lung tissues of infected hamsters measured; XBB.1 loads lower than Delta but comparable to BA.2.75 in oral swabs, and lower than both in lungs.
XBB.1 spreads less efficiently in hamster lungs than BA.2.75, as indicated by lower viral RNA and N-positive cells.
Histopathological scoring of hamster lungs shows XBB.1 causes less damage than Delta, with scores comparable to BA.2.75.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers in vitro growth kinetics, airway-on-chip barrier disruption, in vivo hamster pathogenicity, viral spread, and histopathology, all directly relevant to XBB.1 characterization.

events.summary: Histopathological scoring shows XBB.1 intrinsic pathogenicity lower than Delta and comparable to BA.2.75.
numbers_or_metrics: four out of five histological parameters significantly greater in Delta

events.summary: Phylogenetic analyses suggest XBB emerged via recombination of BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 in summer 2022.
XBB shows substantially higher Re than parental lineages, indicating recombination increased viral fitness.
This is the first documented example of a SARS-CoV-2 variant increasing fitness through recombination rather than substitutions.
XBB.1 shows profound resistance to humoral immunity, with many breakthrough infection sera failing to neutralize it.
Multiple substitutions in XBB.1 S cooperatively contribute to immune resistance, including Y144del in NTD.
XBB acquired two pairs of immune escape-associated and infectivity-enhancing substitutions via one recombination event.
Discrepancy between fusogenicity and intrinsic pathogenicity may be due to non-S mutations, theoretical limits, or hamster model differences.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers histopathology results, discussion of recombination fitness, immune resistance, and limitations.

events.summary: Recombination analysis performed on 562 sequences from GISAID using multiple methods in RDP5.
Phylogenetic analysis of XBB parent lineages and other Omicron variants using Fasttree.
Phylogenies of non-recombinant segments inferred using Iqtree2 after splitting alignment at breakpoint.
Bayesian tip-dated phylogenetic inference for 5' non-recombinant segment using BEAST.
Estimation of relative Re for XBB-related lineages in India using Bayesian multinomial logistic model.
Estimation of epidemic frequencies of XBB and BQ.1 lineages in 56 countries as of Nov 15, 2022.
Hierarchical Bayesian model estimates global and country-specific Re for BQ.1 and XBB lineages.
Hamster model may not reproduce human condition due to potential differences in S protein tropism/affinity.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily describes the methods for recombination, phylogenetic, and epidemic dynamics analyses, with a limitation noted at the end.

events.summary: Plasmids expressing XBB.1 S and BA.2 S-based derivatives were generated by site-directed overlap extension PCR.
SARS-CoV-2 S-based fusion assay performed to measure fusion activity normalized to surface S expression.
Cryo-EM image processing performed in cryoSPARC to reconstruct maps of XBB.1 S states.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for plasmid construction, neutralization assay, virus preparation, yeast surface display, pseudovirus infection, fusion assay, and cryo-EM. These are core experimental procedures supporting the paper's findings.

events.summary: Lung function tests measured Penh and Rpef in hamsters.
methods_or_tools: Buxco Small Animal Whole Body Plethysmography system

events.summary: Immunohistochemistry performed to detect SARS-CoV-2 N protein in lung tissues.
Histopathological scoring of lung damage in infected hamsters.
Multiple regression analysis used to compare experimental conditions over time.
Principal component analysis performed on NT50 values to represent antigenicity.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk primarily describes detailed methods for in vitro and in vivo experiments, including airway-on-a-chip, cell infections, animal experiments, and statistical analyses. It contains no new hypotheses or results, but provides the experimental procedures supporting the paper's findings.
This chunk contains only reference citations and acknowledgements. No research evolution events are described.
This chunk contains only acknowledgments, author contributions, competing interests, and additional information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: In this study, we explored the virological characteristics of XBB, particularly its transmissibility, immune resistance, ACE2 binding affinity, infectivity, fusogenicity, structural information and pathogenicity in a hamster model without a history of vaccination and viral infection (hereafter referred to as intrinsic pathogenicity).
Our phylogenetic analyses suggested that XBB emerged through the recombination of two cocirculating BA.2 lineages, BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 (a progeny of BA.2.75), during the summer of 2022.

Our analysis suggests that the XBB clade's most recent common ancestor (tMRCA) existed at the start of July 2022 (median posterior date: July 7, 2022; 95% HPD confidence intervals: from June 10, 2022, to July 29, 2022).
this is the first documented example of a SARS-CoV-2 variant increasing its fitness through recombination rather than substitutions.
we found that XBB.1 exhibits profound (30-fold) resistance to breakthrough BA.2 infection sera (P=0.0002, Fig. 2a).

several substitutions, such as V83A (2.1-fold, P=0.0034), Y144del (2.9-fold, P=0.0002), Q183E (2.0-fold, P=0.0039), R346T (2.1-fold, P=0.0005), L368I (1.8-fold, P=0.042), V445P (2.1-fold, P=0.0002), F486S (3.0-fold, P=0.0002), and F490S (2.7-fold, P=0.024), conferred significant resistance to breakthrough BA.2 infection sera.
XBB.1 exhibited profound (13-fold) resistance to breakthrough BA.5 infection sera (P<0.0001, Fig. 2b).
our data suggest that multiple substitutions in the XBB.1S cooperatively contribute to the resistance against humoral immunity induced by breakthrough BA.2 infection.
these two mutations (Y144del and G446S) cooperatively contribute to the resistance against humoral immunity induced by breakthrough BA.5 infection.
XBB.1 significantly escaped from monovalent vaccine sera (23.3-fold, P<0.0001), BA.1 bivalent vaccine sera (19-fold, P<0.0001), and BA.5 bivalent vaccine sera (41-fold, P<0.0001) compared with B.1.1.
The antigenicity of XBB.1 is distinct from that of the other Omicron subvariants tested (Fig. 2g).
the R346T substitution, which is common in both XBB.1 and BQ.1.1, significantly increased the binding affinity of BA.2S RBD to human ACE2
the L368I substitution augmented ACE2 binding affinity
the F486S substitution significantly decreased ACE2 binding affinity
the infectivity of the XBB.1 pseudovirus was 7.6-fold greater than that of the BA.2 pseudovirus
two substitutions in the RBD, R346T (1.9-fold) and L368I (2.2-fold), significantly increased pseudovirus infectivity
a substitution in the NTD, V83A (3.3-fold), significantly increased pseudovirus infectivity
two substitutions in the NTD, Y144del (0.18-fold) and G252V (0.54-fold), significantly decreased pseudovirus infectivity
XBB.1 S is significantly more fusogenic than BA.2 S (2.2-fold) and BA.2.75 S (1.5-fold)
two substitutions, V83A and R346T, significantly increased fusogenicity
two substitutions in the RBD (R346T and N460K) and a substitution in the NTD (V83A) contribute to the augmented fusogenicity of XBB.1 S
The XBB.1 S ectodomain was reconstructed as two closed states (closed-1 and closed-2) at resolutions of 2.50 Å and 2.51 Å
The XBB.1 S-ACE2 complex structures were reconstructed by cryo-EM analysis in both RBD one-up and two-up conformations
local refinement was performed on RBD-ACE2 and reconstructed at a resolution of 3.29 Å
the F486S substitution changed the side chain to a less bulky hydrophilic residue and lost hydrophobic interactions with the hydrophobic patch consisting of F28, L79, M82, and Y83 in ACE2
XBB.1 was less replicative than BA.2.75 in this culture system (Fig. 5f).
the percentage of virus that invaded the bottom channel of XBB.1-infected airway-on-chips was significantly higher than that of BA.2.75-infected airway-on-chips (Fig. 5h).
Together with the findings of the S-based fusion assay (Fig. 3d), these results suggest that XBB.1 is more fusogenic than BA.2.75.
the body weights of BA.2.75- and XBB.1-infected hamsters were stable and comparable (Fig. 6a, left).
Delta infection resulted in significant differences in these two respiratory parameters compared to XBB.1 (Fig. 6a, middle and right), suggesting that XBB.1 is less pathogenic than Delta.
the pathogenicity of XBB.1 is comparable to that of BA.2.75.
the viral RNA loads of the hamsters infected with XBB.1 were significantly lower than those infected with Delta, there was no significant difference between XBB.1 and BA.2.75 (Fig. 6b, left).
the viral RNA loads in both the lung hilum and the periphery of XBB.1-infected hamsters were significantly lower than those of BA.2.75- and Delta-infected hamsters (Fig. 6b, middle and right)
the percentage of N-positive cells in the lungs of XBB.1-infected hamsters was significantly lower than that in the lungs of BA.2.75- and Delta-infected hamsters.
the spreading efficiency of XBB.1 in the lungs of infected hamsters is comparable to or even lower than that of BA.2.75.
four out of the five histological parameters as well as the total score of Delta-infected hamsters were significantly greater than those of XBB.1-infected hamsters (Fig. 6e).
the scores of type II pneumocytes, the area of hyperplasia of large type II pneumocytes, and the total histology score of XBB.1-infected hamsters were comparable to those of BA.2.75-infected
four out of the five histological parameters as well as the total score of Delta-infected hamsters were significantly greater than those of XBB.1-infected hamsters
the scores of type II pneumocytes, the area of hyperplasia of large type II pneumocytes, and the total histology score of XBB.1-infected hamsters were comparable to those of BA.2.75-infected hamsters
Our phylogenetic analyses suggested that XBB emerged through the recombination of two co-circulating BA.2 lineages, BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 (a progeny of BA.2.75), during the summer of 2022
XBB shows substantially higher $R_{e}$ than the parental lineages, suggesting that the recombination event increased $R_{e}$ (i.e., viral fitness).
To our knowledge, this is the first documented example of a SARS-CoV-2 variant increasing its fitness through recombination rather than substitutions.
10 out of 14 breakthrough BA.2 infection sera and 9 out of 20 breakthrough BA.5 infection sera fail to neutralize XBB.1.

multiple substitutions in the XBB.1 S protein cooperatively contribute to the immune resistance of XBB.1, and particularly, not only the substitutions in the RBD but also at least a mutation in the NTD, Y144del, closely associates with the immune resistant property of XBB.1.

XBB emerged through recombination in the S gene, and Y144del and V83A are located on the 5' recombinant fragment while F486S and N460K are on the 3' fragment. This means that XBB acquired two pairs of a pair of immune escape-associated and infectivity-enhancing substitutions by only one recombination event.
the intrinsic pathogenicity of XBB.1 was comparable or even lower than that of BA.2.75
certain mutations in the non-S region of the XBB.1 genome can attenuate viral pathogenicity augmented by the higher fusogenicity
a hamster model may not reproduce the human condition
we retrieved a total of 562 sequences satisfying the following criteria from the GISAID database
A number of recombination detection methods were performed on the resulting alignment using the Recombination Detection Program (RDP) v.5.21
only recombination events detected consistently by more than 3 independent methods were retrieved
we retrieved 100 random sequences from each Omicron PANGO lineages: BA.1, BA.2, BA.4 and BA.5 and 20 random sequences from each younger lineage: BQ.1.1, BA.2.75, BJ.1, and BM.1.1.1
we first split the alignment used for the recombination analysis above at genome position 22,920 (the breakpoint inferred by RDP5)
node support was assessed by performing 1000 ultrafast bootstrap replicates
We used a strict molecular clock model with an exponential growth coalescent prior
Parameter estimation was performed via the MCMC approach implemented in CmdStan v2.30.1
Four independent MCMC chains were run with 500 and 1,000 steps in the warmup and sampling iterations
the epidemic frequency of each viral lineage as of November 15, 2022 in each country was estimated
The estimated lineage frequencies for BQ.1 and XBB in each country were shown on the global map
we employed a hierarchical Bayesian multinomial logistic model, which we established in our previous studies
Four independent MCMC chains were run with 500 and 2,000 steps in the warmup and sampling iterations

it might be possible that the tropism and affinity of S proteins of these variants are different between human ACE2 and hamster ACE2, and therefore, a hamster model may not reproduce the human condition
Plasmids expressing the codon-optimized S proteins of XBB.1 and BA.2 S-based derivatives were generated by site-directed overlap extension PCR using the primers listed in Supplementary Table 6.

For calculation of fusion activity, Renilla luciferase activity was normalized to the mean fluorescence intensity (MFI) of surface S proteins. The normalized value (i.e., Renilla luciferase activity per the surface S MFI) is shown as fusion activity.
Two classes of closed states (closed-1 and closed-2) with different RBD orientations were separated in heterogeneous refinement.
The two respiratory parameters (Penh and Rpef) were measured by using a Buxco Small Animal Whole Body Plethysmography system
Immunohistochemical (IHC) analysis (Fig. 6c and Supplementary Fig. 6) was performed as previously described

Pathological features including bronchitis or bronchiolitis, hemorrhage or congestion, alveolar damage with epithelial apoptosis and macrophage infiltration, hyperplasia of type II pneumocytes, and the area of the hyperplasia of large type II pneumocytes were evaluated
a multiple regression analysis including experimental conditions (i.e., the types of infected viruses) as explanatory variables and timepoints as qualitative control variables was performed
Principal component analysis to representing the antigenicity of the S proteins was performed (Fig. 2g).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Investigate the virological characteristics of the SARS-CoV-2 XBB variant, a recombinant lineage from two Omicron subvariants (BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1), to understand how recombination impacts viral fitness, immune evasion, receptor binding, fusogenicity, and pathogenicity, and to explain XBB's rapid global spread.

canonical_events.summary: The study aims to investigate the virological characteristics of the XBB variant, which emerged through recombination of two Omicron subvariants.
Phylogenetic analysis suggests XBB emerged through recombination of BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1 during summer 2022, with a breakpoint in the RBD.
XBB shows higher effective reproduction number (Re) than parental lineages, indicating increased fitness through recombination.
key_numbers: Re of XBB 1.23- and 1.20-times higher than BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1
XBB and XBB.1 Re 1.24- and 1.26-times higher than BA.5

canonical_events.summary: This is the first documented example of a SARS-CoV-2 variant increasing fitness through recombination rather than substitutions.
XBB.1 exhibits profound resistance to neutralization by BA.2 and BA.5 breakthrough infection sera, with many sera failing to neutralize.
key_numbers: 10 out of 14 BA.2 sera fail to neutralize
9 out of 20 BA.5 sera fail to neutralize

canonical_events.summary: Multiple single substitutions in XBB.1 S protein (e.g., V83A, Y144del, F486S, F490S) confer significant resistance to BA.2 and BA.5 sera, with cooperative effects.
XBB.1 significantly escapes from 4-dose vaccine sera, including monovalent, BA.1 bivalent, and BA.5 bivalent vaccines.
XBB.1 is antigenically distinct from other Omicron subvariants, including BQ.1.1, based on hamster sera neutralization and antigenic cartography.
XBB.1 RBD has higher ACE2 binding affinity (KD=1.0 nM) than BA.2 (1.5 nM), with F486S reducing affinity but compensated by other substitutions.
key_numbers: KD: BA.2=1.5 nM, XBB.1=1.0 nM, F486S=2.8 nM

canonical_events.summary: XBB.1 pseudovirus shows 7.6-fold greater infectivity than BA.2 in HOS-ACE2/TMPRSS2 cells, with R346T, L368I, and V83A increasing infectivity.
XBB.1 S is more fusogenic than BA.2 (2.2-fold) and BA.2.75 (1.5-fold) despite lower surface expression, with V83A, R346T, and N460K contributing.
Cryo-EM structures show XBB.1 S predominantly in closed states, with F486S losing hydrophobic interactions with ACE2 and F490S altering interaction with ACE2 K31.
XBB.1 growth kinetics are comparable to BA.2.75 in most cell lines but lower in iPSC-derived alveolar epithelial cells; XBB.1 disrupts airway epithelial-endothelial barriers more than BA.2.75.
In hamsters, XBB.1 causes no weight loss and respiratory parameters comparable to BA.2.75, less pathogenic than Delta; viral RNA loads in lungs lower than BA.2.75 and Delta.
Histopathological scoring shows XBB.1 intrinsic pathogenicity lower than Delta and comparable to BA.2.75.
key_numbers: four out of five histological parameters significantly greater in Delta

canonical_events.summary: XBB acquired two pairs of immune escape-associated and infectivity-enhancing substitutions via one recombination event.
Discrepancy between fusogenicity and intrinsic pathogenicity may be due to non-S mutations, theoretical limits, or hamster model differences.

edges.reason: Defining the problem leads to phylogenetic analysis to understand XBB's origin.
Phylogenetic analysis provides basis for estimating Re and fitness comparison.
Higher Re validates the novel finding of fitness increase via recombination.
Establishing recombination-driven fitness motivates investigation of immune resistance.
Profound resistance leads to dissection of individual substitution contributions.
Understanding substitution effects informs vaccine escape assessment.
Vaccine escape data contributes to antigenic distinctness analysis.
Antigenic distinctness prompts investigation of receptor binding changes.
ACE2 binding affinity data leads to infectivity assessment.
Fusogenicity results prompt structural analysis to explain mechanisms.
Structural insights inform in vitro replication and barrier disruption experiments.
In vitro results lead to in vivo pathogenicity and transmission studies.
In vivo observations are refined by histopathological scoring.
Pathogenicity data contextualizes the recombination-acquired substitution pairs.
The efficient acquisition of beneficial substitutions contrasts with the observed pathogenicity trade-off, leading to limitations.

must_cover_points: Recombination origin of XBB from BJ.1 and BM.1.1.1
Profound immune resistance to BA.2 and BA.5 sera
Cooperative effects of multiple spike substitutions
Vaccine escape from monovalent and bivalent vaccines
Enhanced ACE2 binding affinity despite F486S
Higher fusogenicity despite lower surface expression
Structural basis for altered binding and immune evasion
In vitro growth kinetics and barrier disruption
In vivo pathogenicity comparable to BA.2.75, lower than Delta
Histopathological evidence of reduced intrinsic pathogenicity
Limitations including hamster model and non-S mutations
